introduction: Defined as necrosis of the lung parenchyma, usually caused by microbial infection, often with an air-fluid level. May be classified as acute (symptoms < 1month) or chronic (symptoms > 1month). May also be classified as primary if it occurs in a previously healthy person or in a person prone to aspiration. Secondary lung abscess commonly occurs in association with bronchogenic carcinoma or immunodeficiency states e.g. HIV infection. Lung abscess may be associated with the following • Pyogenic bacteria • Tuberculosis • Fungi • Parasites • Pulmonary infarction • Primary or metastatic malignancies • Silicosis • Coal miner's pneumoconiosis

drug: Metronidazole - Adult: 500 mg orally every 8 hours
Metronidazole - Child: neonate, initially 15 mg/kg orally then 7.5 mg/kg every 12 hours
Metronidazole - Child: 1 month – 12 years: 7.5 mg/kg (maximum 400 mg) every 8 hours
Metronidazole - Child: 12 – 18 years: 400 mg every 8 hours
Amoxicillin - Adult: 500 mg orally every 8 hours for 7 – 10 days
Amoxicillin - Child: less than 5 years: a quarter adult dose
Amoxicillin - Child: 5 – 10 years: half adult dose
Amoxicillin/clavulanic acid - Adult: 1 g/200 mg orally every 8 hours for 7 – 10 days

prevention: Preventive with vaccination in persons at risk
HIV infected patients who are still capable of responding to a vaccine challenge.
